1/ In just one century we transitioned through 1) a corporation age 2) a startup ecosystem age and 3) the crypto ecosystem age. Each is its own superorganism with a different structure of the underlying network.
2/ A corporation is one of humanity’s great inventions. It’s the first organization that could allocate resources around the globe. No other human organization achieved this before.
3/ Compare these two maps: on the left is the largest empire in history, the British Empire. On the right -- a map of countries where you can get Starbucks. https://t.co/3vM8stZBFR

4/ Corporation’s giant reach is possible thanks to the hierarchical structure in which power is formally defined. This lowers the cost of transacting between people who do not know each other -- they need to trust the organization, not themselves.
5/ A corporation’s greatest strength is also its greatest weakness. Its hierarchical structure is designed for scale, not for disruptive innovation. That’s where startup ecosystems come in.
6/ Note that it’s not a startup that’s a big deal here. It’s the startup ecosystem. A startup is more of a product of the ecosystem. It’s an equivalent of a project within a corporation.

7/ Startup ecosystems are non-hierarchical networks built around geo proximity and united in seizing an economic opportunity. Geo proximity --> people know each other --> lower cost of transacting. This replaces the formal structure.
8/ But the feature that makes startups good at innovation also prevents them from scaling. In order to become a global power, a startup needs to become a corporation.

9/ The crypto ecosystem may combine the best of the two worlds: the global scale of a corporation and the ability to innovate like a startup ecosystem. It’s a global-first ecosystem. And it’s the first of its kind.

10/ The crypto ecosystem is a non-hierarchical network built around an idea and united in seizing an economic opportunity. The cost of transacting is lowered in combination by relationships built online and by trustless systems.

11/ This opens a number of questions: is the crypto ecosystem going to replace both corporations and startup ecosystems? Are there going to be “startups” in crypto? And how to track who has influence within this network?
12/ I’ll try to answer these questions during my talk at @ecohconference in Kuwait. Holler if you’re there.

@Esqarrouth @naval My thinking around this topic has been largely inspired by @maciejolpinski’s work.

Think of a group as an algorithm. 

Hierarchical networks try to put the best brain computing machines high in the hierarchy. 

https://t.co/YEBq3914vj

These brains perform computations and produce formulas. We often refer to them as “procedures” or “policies”. 

Lower layers are collecting data and processing it using somebody else’s formulas. 

They are running somebody else’s code.

This mitigates the risk of dramatic miscalculations but forces the network to operate with less information. 

Formulas are being produced with reduced access to data since they are computed only by high-ups. 

Less data → lower accuracy

In a non-hierarchical organization, formulas are shared along with the inputs for the computations that produced them. 

The incentive for sharing is disbursed in attention. 

Attention = influence

Other brains seek out the best formulas and apply them. These formulas can be modified if they turn out to be lacking in the face of new data. 

There is an incentive to improve and share them.

This structure increases the risk of miscalculations. 

Startups come out of non-hierarchical networks -- ecosystems. 

It’s common for a startup to fail. But the network is antifragile -- it can tolerate a high failure rate because it utilizes so much more of the brain compute.

The limitation of non-hierarchical networks lies in scaling. 

The streamlined thinking in hierarchies limits the cognitive load of operating within a large group. 

We cannot cooperate effectively when groups are too large. That’s the reason we form teams.

Dunbar’s number describes this phenomenon. 

It’s often thought of as a limit on how many friends we can have. This is incorrect. 

@simondlr explained it more succinctly than I could: 

https://t.co/3x43cnIyhJ

The cognitive load on tracking relationships within a group grows exponentially with the size of that group.

That’s the reason we gossip. Gossip allows for mitigating the limitations underlined by the Dunbar’s number.

What if we could instead eliminate this problem altogether?

I believe that describing non-hierarchical groups mathematically will allow for removing the scaling limit. 

Then we can outsource this brain function to a machine. As we did with arithmetics and calculators.

How much brain compute power could this free up?

More elegant framework: structured vs. unstructured networks

Structured networks have a hierarchy of power. It's predicated on the formal structure. 

Unstructured networks have a hierarchy of influence. It emerges organically and is predicated on self-interest.

We are living through a comeback to the pseudonymous web. 

It’s easy to forget, but that’s how the internet used to be. 

Introducing real name identities was the innovation in the last boom cycle.

In this video, Reid Hoffman is talking about how real name identity was essential to LinkedIn’s success. 

https://t.co/95a4ZQbpYy

Here Peter Thiel explains Facebook was not the first social media platform. 

It was the first social media platform with real name identities. 

https://t.co/GRdCaYBWdy

Enforcing the use of real name identities is a game theoretical design. It helps improve the average of users behaviors. Users have more at stake. 

It also comes with a cost. Not everybody wants to give up their identity to join.

It makes sense that the anonymity is making its comeback. 

Crypto has given us new tools for designing incentives. 

Preserving privacy may turn out to be the factor of success for the future networks, in the same way as compromising on it was for Linkedin and Facebook.

Nuclear weapons and cryptography have something in common. They both change the economics of conflict. 

Crypto makes it cheaper to defend than attack. 

Nuclear weapons make it economically viable to maintain a destructive arsenal during peacetime.

It's more costly to build a castle than attack a castle. The offense is cheaper than defense. 

But not when it comes to cryptography. It's far cheaper to encrypt than decrypt.

An atomic bomb can wipe out a city in one day. So can conventional bombs. 

But maintaining an arsenal capable of doing that without atomic bombs is prohibitively expensive. 

It's far cheaper to do so with nuclear weapons. Even poor countries can afford that.

Freeman Dyson once observed: 

“The revolutionary thing about the atomic bomb is not that it is so lethal but that it is so cheap.” 

Dyson was inspired by Philip Morrison’s testimony in front of the US Senate where he talked about Tinian island.

There seems to exist a trilemma in decentralized identity analogous to @Zooko's triangle. 

None of the existing solutions are at the same time: 
1) privacy-preserving, 
2) Sybil-resistant 
3) self-sovereign 

They get 2/3 at best. 

Blog post: https://t.co/89qYpocSyo

Thread 👇 https://t.co/KoZQPOkSir
Self-sovereignty — anybody can create and control as many identities as they wish without 3rd party involvement.
Privacy-preserving — one can acquire and utilize an identifier without revealing their ‘real name’ or any other personality identifying information in the process.

Sybil-resistant — identity is subject to scarcity; i.e. creating more identifiers cannot be used to manipulate a system. 

E.g. one cannot gain more voting power this way or gain a higher allocation in a funding round.

Solutions like Telegram Passport or @civickey rely on the KYC process, i.e. nation state infrastructure. They introduce scarcity by compromising privacy and self-sovereignty. The same applies to @poanetwork.

Proof-of-a-Unique-Human relies on biometrics and p2p review process. Questions about security and privacy aside, this approach provides only a single identifier. 

https://t.co/nMTr9qerOE
Web-of-Trust solutions like @SovrinID are promising but lack the source of scarcity. Without it, they are susceptible to Sybil-attacks.

Another promising approach may be derived from decentralized organizations, such as proposed by @joincolony. 

The problem here is that they first would have to become massively popular.

Finally, I'm curious to see where the ideas around a social graph of personal cryptocurrencies such as @CirclesUBI go to. 

The value of social connections gets priced because each connection offers upside, but also carries the risk for both sides.

Zooko's triangle was solved with blockchain. PoW provided an abstraction of financial capital. 

Decentralized Identity Trilemma would be trivial to solve if there was an objective metric providing an abstraction for human capital.
@FreeTrade68 @SovrinID That may be so, but as long as the attack is possible, that's a problem.

Identity needs to be nested in the scarcity of human capital.

1/ Here is another way to think about this:

It's a struggle between Power & Influence

Both are a result of technological revolutions: 
1) Mass-media: scalable 1-way communication (printing press, radio, TV)
2) Social-media: scalable 2-way communication (the internet) https://t.co/9FNvvMetrr

2/ Mass media is power-driven. 

Those at the top of the hierarchy have a monopoly over broadcasting. Everybody listens. 

The winning strategy: say things that people who can threaten your position in the hierarchy like.

3/ Social media is influence-driven. 

Anybody can broadcast, but it's not a given somebody listens. 

The winning strategy: deliver the message that your audience likes.

4/ Social media is bound to win. This is a hard nut to crack for those that control mass media. 

They try to employ the strategy that succeeded before to this new medium. I.e. control the hierarchy that controls the platform. 

But that’s not so easy.

5/ 
The currency of mass media is political capital. 

The currency of social media is social capital.

On "scientist" vs. "ideologues" mindset: 

* Scientist mindset: asks how this view can be falsified? 
* Ideologue mindset: asks what this view tells me about speaker's loyalties?

We all seem to use both on different occasions. I certainly have noticed this about myself.

How to tell if you have an "ideologues mindset"? 

I noticed that I fall into it when I feel insecure and/or scared. 

It happens when somebody undermines a viewpoint that I never attempted to question AND it's convenient for me to share it.

How to tell if you have a "scientist mindset"? 

It's simple. Ask yourself: "What would have to be true to change my view on this?"

If you do not know the answer to this question right away, then you don't.

Aluminum was once more expensive than gold.

In 1855 aluminum ingots were displayed at an exhibition organized at the bequest of French emperor Napoleon III. 

The metal became an instant hit with the French elites. It was shiny and light; the prices skyrocketed.

Henri Sainte-Claire Deville who developed a method of producing aluminum at a large scale was reportedly infuriated with its popularity with the elites. 

He correctly believed that if the prices were not inflated, the metal could have found more practical uses.

So what about gold? It clearly has lots of practical applications. Despite its price, it's used in:

The problem with the current payment infrastructure is that it's managed by organizations that can be infiltrated by ideological movements. 

Worse so, it's an effective & economically viable strategy. Thread 👇 https://t.co/ZbyPaq4d8E

Payment companies capture a relatively small amount of value compared to what their service adds to the economy. 

Consequently refusing their service causes more harm to targeted parties than to the payment processor itself.

Exerting pressure on payment companies to ban an ideological opponent is cheap and deadly. 

The cost for the payment processors is marginal - they keep their market share and stay profitable. 

The opponent likely goes out of business.

A powerful ideological movement can co-opt all major payment companies. This effectively creates a *monopoly* 

None of the companies is a monopolist - they still compete with each other. But the ideological movement gets to selectively ban people from the system.

It's scary how easy it is to infiltrate and control a hierarchical organization like a corporation. 

You don't need to change the CEO. 

Just control key roles & departments that allow exerting power over individuals across the organization.

If I was trying to infiltrate a corporation, I'd start with the HR department. 

I'd make sure that it's stuffed with enough members of the movement, so that it affects the hiring pipeline. 

This would gradually bring in more believers in the ideology.

In the next step, the movement would likely capture the parts of the organization that is responsible for its policy and PR. 

At some point, the CEOs hands are tied.

You think it's a conspiracy theory? This happened before. 

For instance, white supremacists infiltrated law enforcement and military. 

Source: https://t.co/IWhrNhs661

Infiltrating tech companies can be more dangerous than police or military. Tech is scalable. 

I think there is a reasonable chance this has happened. But even if not, it should terrify all of us that it could.

Crypto communities are like city-states (Athens, Sparta, early Rome). 

Each has its own economy, leadership, currency and... an army. 

City-states fought over territory. Crypto communities fight over use-cases.

A community is a network. 

As such, it will share certain properties regardless of whether it emerges around geographical proximity (e.g. a village) or an idea (e.g. an open-source project).

.@Iiterature @jessewldn and @ali01 from @a16z Crypto famously compared crypto networks to cities. 

https://t.co/SmZGfMD2IU

They do share some commonalities, but this analogy does not hold under scrutiny.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z From the population perspective, both cities and crypto networks are complex systems. I.e. they have emergent properties at different levels of scale. 

https://t.co/nczXh5t4S8

More units are added to the hierarchy as the city scales.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Crypto communities and city-states are complex systems both from the population and administrative perspective. 

Rome’s administrative system evolved different models as it scaled. So does Ethereum.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Leadership in crypto communities is fluid. 

You might be a member of the ‘ruling elite’ (protocol developers, large miners etc.), but your influence will change at different points in time. As it did for a Roman senator.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Cities don’t have enemies. NYC and Washington DC might be competitors, but its citizens don’t fight each other. 

Sparta and Athens were regularly at war. So are crypto communities.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Citizens double down as soldiers. Both in city-states and crypto communities. 

An Athenian farmer turned into a hoplite when the city was in danger. An otherwise friendly developer joins a Twitter mob bashing an opposing group when network’s interest is at stake.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Cities don’t have their own currencies. They are not economically independent. 

Independent city-states had their own coinage. So do crypto communities.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Currency plays an even more important role in crypto communities than it did for city-states.  

There is no land ownership here to bind citizens interests. It all revolves around the currency.

@Iiterature @jessewldn @ali01 @a16z Cities don’t demand loyalty. If you move from Munich to Berlin, you will have the same rights as those who grew up there.

I wrote a short whitepaper for the upcoming #RebootingWebOfTrust event: 

“Attention as a Source of Scarcity for Decentralized Identity Systems”

Full version here: https://t.co/CjoElRQVHP

Tweetstorm version 👇

Attention is a basic component of all cognitive processes. It is also limited and therefore is a natural source of scarcity. 

By quantifying attention flows between identities, we can introduce scarcity without sacrificing privacy or self-sovereignty. https://t.co/k0SG3xtDwh

Quantifying attention flows between identities offers a solution to Decentralized Identity Trilemma. 

https://t.co/uYlOXdm8Wd

When scarcity is nested in quantified attention flows, there is:

1) no requirement of providing any ‘real name’ documentation or credentials, 

2) no hindering users ability to create and control identifiers including any limitation in terms of how many can be created.

Attention plays a similar role in the brain that electricity plays in a computer. 

Both the computer and the brain can perform various types of computations. However, all of them require electricity and attention respectively. https://t.co/p08uOaAdm0

I can walk on a slackline, look for a pattern in a string of numbers or plan what to cook for dinner. 

My brain is flexible enough to perform each of these tasks, but they require attention. 

I can switch between them, but I cannot do them simultaneously.

Making up an idea about somebody is another type of computation that our brain performs. It is subject to the same limitation as other cognitive processes. 

We dedicate different amounts of attention to people depending on how important we perceive them to be.

A person with the highest status in a group is going to receive a lot of attention. 

This is true in a boardroom, in a bar, at a conference or on Twitter. 

The algorithm behind https://t.co/VxMbe6fQNl is based on this principle. 

https://t.co/R5wQWD6bh7

E.g. government spending is transparent for the most part and it’s not like we all agree on it. 
 
https://t.co/X5l4Bbb6kk

2/This point is more important than open-sourcing algorithms. The key is to create infrastructure which enables users to choose which algorithm is curating their information feed. 
 
https://t.co/nDXIHxQzxy
3/The problem is that the companies controlling social media platforms are becoming a weak link in our society's governance model.

4/The more power concentrates in the companies operating social media platforms, the more attractive targets they become.
 
It is a no-brainer for ideologies seeking to subjugate other groups to try to take control over them.

5/ A coup to take over a state starts with capturing government buildings and tv/radio stations.
 
The coup for the control over the internet meta-state starts with capturing information feeds.

6/ A state coup happens rapidly & violently. The coup over the internet meta state is going to happen slowly & meticulously. They will boil the frog.
 
But this can be prevented.
7/ We need to decentralize control over information feeds. In order to do that we need to create shared data, identity and social graph layers.
8/ Social media platforms as we know them today should become simply a UI + curation layer.
9/ Social media companies would command a fraction of the power they have today if users could easily switch between them.

10/ If a user wants to leave a social media platform the cost is high for them and marginal for the platform: they need to start over somewhere else and the platform keeps all the graph data. https://t.co/T6vkm0o3xo

11/ If social media platforms become merely UIs this dynamic reverses.
 
Suddenly, it's costly for the platform to lose a user, but the cost for the user is marginal: they can find the same service somewhere else without needing to rebuild their graph. https://t.co/7Z84PoFqbi

12/ People who talk about breaking up tech companies don't understand the problem. Breaking up the company does not address the root cause: the platforms have leverage over its users. 

The only solution is decentralization.

The Bitcoin ecosystem is bound to develop its own venture capital. It is likely to be as activist for libertarian causes as Silicon Valley’s capital is for progressive ones. 

Blog: https://t.co/aaFwbjI5k1
🧵👇

Today, there are relatively few venture bets being made by Bitcoiners, despite the large amounts of capital they control. Most believe that even successful investments would underperform BTC; HODLing has a better ROI.
If Bitcoin is to develop its own economy, it will need some form of high-risk capital. This capital is eventually going to emerge, as long as the market cap keeps growing.
There are already hedge-funds operating in such a way, i.e., their returns are denominated in BTC. For example, @AdamantCapital

Trading is fundamentally different from VC. VCs take longer-term positions. So, they have to find investments that will outperform BTC over 5 years, rather than 5 weeks or 5 days. It is not surprising that we have not seen funds that would promise that. Yet...

We are likely to see BTC-denominated VC funds due to a combination of two functions:

1) The slowing down of BTC’s price growth in relative terms 
2) Growth in investment opportunities in companies charging their fees relative to BTC https://t.co/sGLpD4sOkm
Past the inflection point, the best way to accumulate BTC will no longer be HODLing, but rather investing in businesses that benefit from the growth of the Bitcoin economy.

The Venture Era of Bitcoin is going to have social implications. Allocating capital is a form of influence. It can and often is used to further ideological causes. 

More on influence: https://t.co/Ysr4qKympa

Silicon Valley is predominantly progressive, and so is it’s VC. This is manifested in the many firms actively seeking to invest in startups working on solutions to global warming or pushing for gender diversity on boards of directors.
Silicon Valley VCs generally don’t fund projects catering to the military. Surely there is money to be made there, so this seems to reflect an ideological choice.

There are, of course, conservative VCs in Silicon Valley. Nonetheless, political correctness is defined by the dominant ideology, in this case progressivism. Breaking the ideological dogma would result in a high reputational cost, e.g. losing access to many rounds.

It is reasonable to expect that similar dynamics will emerge in the Bitcoin VC market. Consequently, it is likely that investors will support libertarian causes, such as the preservation of individual freedoms, as much as Silicon Valley VCs support progressive ones.

Bitcoin can be defined both as a subset and a superset of Crypto. 

Which one is the superset depends on whether the sets are defined by resources or by liquidity. https://t.co/QL9vBsf5EM

If the sets are defined as resources (people, machines etc.) then Bitcoin is a subset of Crypto. 

If the sets are defined by liquidity then Crypto is a subset of Bitcoin (no other coin would exist without BTC's liquidity).

This was helpful for me to understand why some Bitcoiners (h/t @wiz) talked about Crypto being part of Bitcoin and not the other way around. 

My natural instinct was to think through the lens of resources. But the liquidity-first view makes a lot of sense, too.
@BitcoinErrorLog @wiz The issue of using the word "crypto" is that it seems to be a catchall phrase. This framework offers a more precise language.

Politics is going horizontal. It is not who you vote for anymore, but where do you shop. What you buy or don't buy. Which companies do you work or don't work for. Who you take capital from or which companies do you invest in.

We are abandoning the world of vertical politics, where you choose your local representatives. Your choice has second order effects in the higher levels of the stack (national, regional, international politics), but your daily life can be largely apolitical.

In the world of horizontal politics the ideological war rages at all levels. Politics is no longer bottoms up. It is now tops down. Global ideological war has a bigger impact on your life than local political battles.
I noticed that I've been paying attention to the US politics, while I pay no attention to politics in Europe – where I grew up and still work and live.
I'm afraid that it is going to be harder to opt out from politics in this new world. If you have any influence sooner or later the battling parties will demand you take a side.

Corporations signaling belonging to an ideological tribe is a sign of the world to come. Depending on your political preferences you may boycott or spend money with Nike, Gillette or Ben & Jerries to make a point.

All commercial brands have the same goal – to be profitable. They would pursue it by decisions in positioning as: 
* affordable
* luxurious 
* great service 
etc. 

Tribal affiliation is becoming a new dimension of brand positioning. We will probably see more examples of that.

Two sets of agents play a series of games for a cumulative reward. The goal of the competition is to find the optimal configuration of features for the environment.
This algorithm is quite commonplace. For example, the mating between Startups and VCs is also governed by the same algorithm.

Males (Startups) can easily tell which Females are the most attractive and compete over them 

Females (VCs) cannot easily tell which Males are the most attractive and the attractiveness is partly predicated on other Females’ choices

Men & Women are trying to mate with the most attractive member of the opposite sex to maximize propagation of their genes. 

Startups & VCs are trying to mate with the most attractive member of the opposite sex to maximize value of their assets.
Founders dressing down looks like Fisherian runaway https://t.co/u08hYRnWVb https://t.co/1ZdJHPfV99

Startup/VC world has also its equivalent of what Dawkins called a He-man theory, i.e. once Females see that other Females see a given Male as the most attractive, they start competing over him. I.e. the top Male takes all. https://t.co/5nC5DJMmdQ

The justification for the He-man theory is that a big part of what makes the top Male attractive is that it is likely to produce offspring that future Females will find attractive. So the opinion of other agents matters a lot.

The same dynamic works with startups. A big part of what makes a hot Startup attractive is that it will likely attract more capital from other investors in the future. So the opinion of other agents matters a lot, too.
@ljxie The bottom line is that they compete over the same resources.

The problem is not that social media platforms take sides.

@benedictevans It sounds like we have different ideas of what this means. 

By opening up a graph I mean being able to replicate my following on another platform easily AND being able to communicate with people who remained on the platform I left.
@benedictevans Sure, but only if each user made this information explicitly public. This is the case for most users, e.g. on Twitter. And yet, we can't freely move follows & tweets between platforms.
Today platforms are vertically structured: they both have the UI and the graph (data about your relationships with other users). Each platform is its own stack.
The graph is what's valuable in a platform. The information about your relationship with other users.
When you want to leave Facebook or Twitter you don't get to take YOUR graph with you. They get to keep it, you have to start over.
This creates an unbalanced relationship between you and the platform. The platform has all the leverage. https://t.co/W3jCQLutwo
The solution to problems with social media platforms is simple: move the leverage on the side of the user. Then platforms will need to work hard to keep you happy. https://t.co/xc38Ge6lcr

Most people view this election as: 

*Republican vs. Democrat * Trump vs. Biden * Right vs. Left*

I think you can gain more insight from viewing it instead as 

*Anti-mainstream vs. Mainstream*
In order to use this framework you have to ignore the candidates and only focus on what they represent: Mainstream vs. Anti-mainstream.

What is mainstream? It's what comes from the Elites. 

Who are the Elites? Those at the top of hierarchies that control key gatekeepers: 
political parties, 
media, 
tech companies, 
academic journals & universities 
etc.

In politics: DNC, RNC

Democrats had anti-elite candidates, too (Sanders, Yang, Gabbard), but they got squashed by the DNC.

Trump not only won DESPITE the elites, he subjugated them. Republicans who kept opposing him are now marginalized.

In media: NYT, CNN, FOX News

If putting these together doesn't make sense to you then you're still not using the framework. 

Yes, FOX News supports Trump, but at the beginning they attacked him. The more they criticized him, the more their ratings dropped. Trump subjugated it.

In business: Google, Facebook 

Business elites are those with the most leverage. 

Capital is cheap today. What matters is who controls attention flows.

In science: academic journals, universities admin

One makes a career in academia by publishing in prestigious journals and associating with prestigious institutions. Those controlling these gatekeepers decide what's mainstream science.

"Believe the science" is the opposite of what science stands for: evidence, not belief. 

But when Biden says it it is not a contradiction. He doesn't mean "follow the scientific method", he literally means "believe to the scientific elites".

@ErikVoorhees @saratoga733 The problem here is not that they make their own decisions, but that they hold the graph hostage. This takes away from the consumer the free choice of a provider.
Most of the discussions about social media power miss the critical distinction: between the platform and the graph.

The reason we're in this mess is that there is no separation between social media platforms and the graph. 

Twitter, Facebook etc. control both the platform and the graph.

The social graph are the connections that we make with each other. This is not property of any company. And yet, you can't freely move this information between different platforms, which means that the platforms effectively own it. That's crazy when you think about it.

The equivalent of what goes on with SM platforms would be a club, where you are not allowed to exchange contact information with people you meet there. 

You're free to leave, but then you have to somehow find the people you want to stay in touch with. Not impossible, but hard.
This is how SM works today. If you leave Twitter, FB etc. you have to reconstruct your graph MANUALLY somewhere else. This creates a massive barrier and gives platform huge LEVERAGE.
The answer to this problem is not "breaking up Big Tech". That's silly – it doesn't even address the issue!

The key is to enable freely moving between platforms. I.e. it needs to be easy and quick to replicate your graph on other platforms. It has to be automated. It needs to happen with a click of a button.

When this is the case then a platform banning somebody for WHATEVER reason is fine. If you don't like it you CAN leave. 

Right now you can, but you really can't. You have to incur a massive cost to do so. That's the reason people freak out. They are right to do so.

Luckily, the technology to do that already exists – cryptographic proofs. 

You can post a cryptographic proof on your SM accounts, website, via email etc. This proves that the same entity controls all of them. 

Copies of these proofs can be stored & verified by anyone.
.@KeybaseIO was the first project to bring cryptographic proofs to a large number of users. But there are more projects that enable that. My personal favorite is @keyoxide.
With cryptographic proofs we can build a universal & decentralized social graph that's not owned or controlled by any single entity.

With a Universal Social Graph there can be any number of services that enable you to back up your network. 

If you want to move from one platform to another you can find all the people you were previously connected to with a click of a button. And they can find you.

Creation of a Universal Social Graph would change the imbalance of power between the user and the platform. 

Today it's in favor of the platforms. With USG it would be in favor of the user. You.

Twitter should start charging for handles 

They'd make more money while improving the relationship with users and startups in their ecosystem 

🧵👇 or blog post on https://t.co/0940l5W7SZ
Twitter is sleeping on a goldmine –– it can be the "ICANN of social media" because it is already a de facto identity system for a significant part of the internet

Twitter would make $4.8b a year if they charged $12/year for a handle to their ~400m users 

(it costs $12 to renew a .com domain) 

For comparison, their total revenue for 2021 was $5.1b
Twitter could also make money from auctioning off new names and taking a commission from secondary market sales
Buying a .com for $100k? Additional $10k for a matching Twitter handle doesn't sound crazy to me

People do not like to pay for things they used to get for free, but you do not own your Twitter handle when you do not pay for it 

12$/year is an excellent deal for more control over your identity
Twitter is struggling with spam, impersonations, and scams, and these issues would mostly go away if the platform charged for handles
Users could still create free accounts, but their handles would be their ID: a string of numbers – hard to remember and not prestigious

Relying on advertising revenue puts Twitter at odds with startups building its ecosystem  

Advertising makes up >85% of Twitter's revenue, so they can't tolerate startups "stealing eyeballs"

Startups building on Twitter’s social graph and the naming system add value to the Twitter platform 

Twitter needs a new business model to embrace them

Imagine you can have your Twitter handle automatically reserved for you on Clubhouse, Substack, and elsewhere

+ consistent branding for you 
+ less complexity for startups 
+ more money for Twitter (from selling handles)

Imagine you can automatically follow the same people on Clubhouse that you follow on Twitter (and the other way around) 

Every startup building on Twitter’s social graph would expand it, making it even more valuable
Twitter needs to embrace the role of the social graph as a platform
An identity-based business model may be the way to go for Twitter

The state should be a service provider, not a sovereign

The state's power stems from 3 monopolies:

1) physical violence
2) money 
3) identity

Take away 1/3 and you weaken the power imbalance, but the state still rules, not serves 

Take away 2/3 and the state becomes a service provider

The monopoly on physical violence is not going away 

Bitcoin is breaking the monopoly on money 

This leaves identity

The state issues identifiers such as passports, IDs, social security numbers, etc. 

As you go through life various credentials (e.g. degrees, certificates) get associated with those identifiers 

Without them, you can't function in the society

I've been researching how to decentralize social media for years.

#nostr is the first approach I thought, "wow, this can work!" and I'm getting more bullish by the day

Here are a few reasons:

#1 reason: #nostr is dead simple

* Everyone runs a client
* Users have a private & pub key
* Users post by signing a message and sending it to multiple relays
* Relays provide the convenience of a centralized service
* There are multiple relays, so getting banned isn't a problem

#2 reason: #nostr allows the use of external identifiers

Mastodon's fatal flaw is that handles are tied to instances; you get banned by an instance and lose your identifier; it's just a worse Twitter.

You can use, e.g., your domain name in #nostr

https://t.co/rN924OgoBC

#3 reason: relays run SQL databases (think: fast & cheap!)

That means that social media platforms built on top of #nostr will be as fast and cheap to maintain as centralized platforms are today.

#4 reason: traction without funding

#nostr didn't raise any money; there is no company or a token. @fiatjaf just put up a short doc on GitHub and started coding

https://t.co/DktyAPzAR2

#5 reason: there is an organic and thriving community

It started with a telegram group @fiatjaf created. No marketing budget and no community managers.

There are +1k people in the group, and prob ~200 are regularly active.

https://t.co/hUG1NMAaun

#7 reason: it already works

https://t.co/VLYdZg0o8g

#8 reason: #nostr doesn't use blockchain

#nostr doesn't need blockchain to work. There are many Bitcoiners in the #nostr community, but you can use #nostr without ever needing to use Bitcoin.
@decentmoney2009 For an end user I’d recommend @damusapp by @jb55

#9 reason: you can build a business on #nostr

Client & relay operators can make money, e.g.:

* Charge fees in BTC or any other cryptocurrency 
* Run ads
* Sell handles 
etc.
@former_earther @bitstein https://t.co/2KqaEsMrJQ

#Nostr & @farcaster_xyz share many similarities; the biggest difference lies in the philosophies they represent.

As you will see, that's the same philosophical difference that separates Bitcoin & Ethereum.

Let me explain

First, let's talk about how #Nostr & @farcaster_xyz are alike:

1. Everyone runs a client and signs messages with a private key

2. Both use relays (or hubs), which store copies of signed messages

3. You could move Twitter to either of them without users noticing
If you spend time with early adopters of #nostr & @farcaster_xyz, you will quickly notice that the first group is all Bitcoiners, and the second is all Ethereans.

The founder of #nostr @fiatjaf is anon. He put up a doc online & started coding; no company, no funding, no team.

The founder of @farcaster_xyz @dwr is well-connected in SV, has raised money, created a company, and hired a team.

Rings a bell?
The two most important ways in which #nostr and @farcaster_xyz differ are their approaches to identity & relaying messages.

Do you see the pattern already?

#Nostr believes in bottom-up emergence and optimizes for max decentralization. @farcaster_xyz believes in intelligent design and optimizes for fast adoption.

I think that the same philosophical divide runs between Bitcoin and Ethereum.

.@farcaster_xyz UX is  *excellent*. None of #nostr clients come close; it's even better than Twitter.

It's a great product, and I can see it taking off.

(notice the attention to detail: (GM) button replacing likes on posts with "GM" or showing pfps next to no. of replies) https://t.co/o7Vyr7fTui

The architecture @farcaster_xyz picked enables it to replicate Twitter faster but limits its ability to adopt other formats.

#Nostr can support *every* social media format, and integrating *existing* platforms is shockingly easy

https://t.co/T7nRISzctN

Platforms can't force you to see ads in a dec social stack because you can switch providers. This means that value will accrue elsewhere.

The namespace is the best candidate, in my opinion.

https://t.co/0940l5W7SZ

If the value accrues in the namespace, then capital will flow into the id space with many new solutions.

#Nostr can adopt whatever turns out to be the winning id system, and the probability of it being Ethereum is low.

#Nostr can't be censored. Running a relay is so cheap that even if you get banned by others, you can run your own easily.

Running @farcaster_xyz's hub is estimated to cost thousands $/year -- a significant barrier to entry.

https://t.co/9gfPQ7vlkf

The cost will show up somewhere. Running a relay in #nostr is cheap, but this makes devs' job harder (think: slower & costlier).

@farcaster_xyz is less censorship-resistant, but building on the protocol is faster and cheaper.

I suspect that @farcaster_xyz, which aims to be "sufficiently decentralized," may benefit from #nostr's censorship resistance.

Kind of like Ethereum and other coins benefit from Bitcoin's decentralization. It's worth less attacking them if users can just move to Bitcoin.

#Nostr is compatible with all blockchains but does not rely on any.

Projects promising to put "tweets on the blockchain" raised hundreds of millions of dollars in venture capital, but this is a terrible idea.

Tweets have short half-time life, and storing them on a blockchain has the inverse economics to what’s needed.

Content posted on social media is only relevant for a brief time. For example, in my experience, a tweet gets ~90% of its lifetime engagement in the first 48 hours. While the value of old messages declines, the cost of storage in a blockchain network stays constant.

Advocates of “using blockchain” often discuss the benefits of payments, NFTs, etc., but this makes no sense. A protocol not using blockchain for storing messages can still e.g. integrate lightning for payments.
NFTs are a genuine innovation. You've got to give credit to Ethereum for that.

The adoption of decentralized social media will bring about the end of the nation-state.

(It's a working hypothesis, please poke holes)

cc @balajis @lopp @tuurDemeester
TLDR; the changes in communication tech have brought about changes in the belief & political systems in the past; the same should happen now.
Luther's Reformation followed ~2 orders of magnitude reduction in the cost of books. https://t.co/gb11rgA9h4
While both the Catholic church and the Reformers had access to this technology, the challenger (Lutherans) took advantage of it more effectively. https://t.co/0JBgJu9N2y
In 1848, there was a wave of anti-monarchic revolutions in Europe ("The Spring of Nations). https://t.co/Smk1vvBCuM

The Revolutions of 1848 were preceded by the invention of the cast iron printing press – it reduced the force needed to operate the press by 90% and 2x-ed the printed area size.

(I assume ~ an order of magnitude improvement in cost, but I couldn't find data to back it up) https://t.co/bL0lbZ3mCt

Continued innovation in printing tech led to mass media.

Mass Media was to the nation-state what religion was to the monarchy – it legitimized it.

The internet was the next tech revolution, which brought about orders of magnitude reduction in the cost of disseminating information.

Mass Media, the nation-state's old ally, got replaced by Big Tech. https://t.co/uKiOjpL59P

Big Tech is under the government's thumb (openly in China and covertly in the West); it's like Lutherans replacing Catholics – it may be less corrupt, but it still legitimizes the current form of government.

Decentralized social media will be outside of the government's control. https://t.co/tjvasxnHUZ

We got a taste of what's coming during the Arab Spring, where Western social media platforms played a key role in coordinating protests across the region.

The social media platforms were outside of the control of the Arab governments.
https://t.co/2kTkLrblYx

Decentralized social media will bring about a step change in the share of human interactions that can be indexed and thus used for mass coordination.

Two drivers:
1) Open access to the social graph
2) Permissionless innovation (explosion of new social apps and formats)M https://t.co/SObazk0hSX

History shows that when there is a step change in communication cost, a change in power structures follows, and the old power doesn't go without a fight.

(The Catholic church persecuted printers and destroyed their presses. The media has been vilifying Big Tech)

Wow 

Twitter is adding a LinkedIn-like affiliation graph 

This is huge, let me explain https://t.co/TErOgno7oE

You can boil down Linkedin's value prop to 3 core pieces: 

1) Connections "who you know" 
2) Affiliations "who you associate with" 
3) CV "what you've done"

Linkedin Connections used to be valuable because you could easily discover who could make intros ("mutual connection"). 

This feature became unusable because the platform incentivized adding more connections without removing any.

Twitter's follow graph is better than Linkedin's connections because users actively curate their follows – not only add but also remove them. 

There are already 3rd party tools that offer Linkedin-like functionality on top of Twitter.
So far, Twitter has been missing an important piece – information about who works were. This is about to change; no more googling people you meet on Twitter to find their LinkedIn profiles.

Finally, adding affiliations creates a de facto CV from each user profile. This is what made Linkedin a shelling point for recruiters and job seekers. 

$$$

* Twitter will become a better biz dev platform 
* Twitter will compete with Linkedin for the lucrative recruiting market 
* Twitter will have a Web of Trust between accounts, i.e., it helps with impersonations & spam bots 

But I'm excited about something else...

Linkedin has monopolized and kept milking the affiliations graph for ~20 years, stalling innovation on the internet. 

They closed their API and sued startups that tried to scrape the public profiles – they lost but killed plenty of companies. 

https://t.co/kQTMydVUES

Many startups tried to disrupt Linkedin, but the network effects were too strong. 

Twitter is not a startup, though. 

It already has network effects and visionary management, which Linkedin lacks.

Twitter can start a new wave of innovation on the internet if they combine interest + affiliation graphs and make them available via API. 

Exciting times @elonmusk

@eladgil Source https://t.co/yyKQtwQMwz

Lots of predictions on what the impact of Larger Language Models (LLMs) is going to be, so here is mine: 

Our brains will shrink in size, literally. https://t.co/hIwlJQJQRZ

First, let's talk about the brain. The brain is *expensive* 

It consumes energy at 10x the rate of the rest of the body per gram of tissue; it is responsible for ~2% of body weight but ~20% of all energy use.

We evolved to have larger brains because the benefits outweigh the cost. But 3k years ago, there was a sharp drop in brain size. 

Wait, what... shouldn't it be the other way around? Exactly. https://t.co/DzpXWONEWk

Writing was invented around the same time. It's a profound invention because it enables saving information outside the brain. 

How could learning how to write affect the size of the brain? It didn't, but it did change our social structures.

Writing enabled building larger societies, i.e., more specialization. 

We became more effective at sharing – and more dependent on – the collective mind. 

https://t.co/yyKQtwQMwz

What does it have to do with LLMs? They offer a path towards utilizing our collective mind more effectively, too. 

I.e., you will be more dependent on the collective, but your ability to solve problems will expand.
When two people communicate, they attempt to transfer the states of their neural nets. We can't do that directly, so we compress our ideas into words, pictures, songs, stories, etc.

The better the compression, the faster and more reliable the transfer. But that's where it becomes tricky. In reality, what works best is "tailor-made" compression. 

E.g. compare watching a lecture vs. 1-1 tutoring.

The rate at which our conscious minds can process information is fixed at 40bit/s. The only way to bypass this limit is better compression. 

https://t.co/1jJMA1l1Js

ChatGPT is *not* a brain; it's an interface to the brain. So is DALL-E, Midjourney, etc. 

Even though these products appear as if they could think, what they really do is compression. 

And that's huge.

Nostr -- layers instead of silos https://t.co/uKvaToGIuM

Platforms like Twitter or Facebook are silos with clients, algos, servers, identifiers all bundled together.  

Nostr is a protocol that treats each one of these elements as an interoperable piece that can have multiple providers. 

This way, they become layers in a stack.

Viewed from information theory perspective, Bitcoin provides error correction mechanism for money. 

Modern financial system has low latency and high throughput, but suffers from high error rate (e.g. money printing)

There is no need for any technical integration, just liquidity. 

As long as other forms of money can be easily exchanged for Bitcoin, the error rate is going to be gradually reduced.
